data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Elite ideological closure in competitive gift-exchange systems generated runaway status competition that exhausted palace revenues and legitimated peripheral predation simultaneously", "mechanism": "Palace elites in Greece, Anatolia, and Levant maintained power through conspicuous consumption and gift-exchange networks requiring ever-larger bronze, luxury craft, and exotic good flows; ca. 1200 BCE combination of tin supply interruption (Afghanistan/Anatolia mines) and elite demographic expansion created structural contradiction where (a) tribute extraction intensified on peasant base, causing internal flight/defection, and (b) excluded peripheral elites (maritime traders, mercenary captains, provincial governors) transformed into predatory 'Sea Peoples' to access prestige goods circuit; Egypt survived because pharaonic ideology had earlier shifted to mortuary temple endowments and divine kingship spectacle—less materially intensive and more ideologically self-sustaining than competitive peer-polity gift regimes", "scope": {"covers": ["Elite consumption pressure", "Materialist underpinning of ideological systems", "Endogenous social dynamics", "Egyptian ideological alternative"], "excludes": ["Climate/environmental prime movers", "Population pressure on carrying capacity (peasant-level Malthusianism)", "Technical/technological factors"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Linear B tablets show stable or declining palace craft production quotas through 1200 BCE, not intensification", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian New Kingdom royal tomb construction and mortuary temple endowment expenditures exceeded Mycenaean/Hittite elite consumption as share of agricultural surplus", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Sea Peoples ethnic identifications in Egyptian texts match palace-dependent populations (displaced Mycenaean/Hittite officials) rather than peripheral maritime groups", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Archaeometric analysis shows stable tin-bronze supply lines to all palace centers through 1180 BCE without isotopic sourcing shift", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Highly criticizable: prioritizes Pierre Bourdieu/anthropological economy over climate or military determinism. Treats ideology as material practice. Atypical in centering elite competition rather than peasant subsistence or external invasion."}
